---
title: "Exploratory Text Analysis"
---

## Starting with Description

Before modelling, describe the corpus: size, vocabulary richness, most frequent terms, temporal trends. Exploratory analysis generates hypotheses and catches preprocessing errors.

## Word Frequencies

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulated news corpus
set.seed(42)
words <- c("media", "government", "policy", "election", "party",
           "leader", "vote", "minister", "public", "economy",
           "climate", "health", "debate", "report", "journalist")

corpus_sim <- tibble(
  doc  = sample(1:50, 500, replace = TRUE),
  word = sample(words, 500, replace = TRUE, prob = c(8,7,6,6,5,5,4,4,4,3,3,3,2,2,1))
)

corpus_sim |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |>
  ggplot(aes(n, reorder(word, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(x = "Frequency", y = NULL, title = "Top 10 terms")
```

## Lexical Diversity

Lexical diversity measures vocabulary richness relative to text length:

```{webr}
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)

docs <- c(
  tabloid = "The star hit the fan over the big money deal after the big star hit back at the big club.",
  broadsheet = "Parliamentary scrutiny of executive decisions intensified following leaked correspondence."
)

corp <- corpus(docs)
toks <- tokens(corp)

# Type-Token Ratio
textstat_lexdiv(dfm(toks), measure = "TTR")
```

## Keyness: Comparing Two Corpora

Keyness identifies terms that are significantly more frequent in one corpus versus a reference corpus — useful for comparing outlets, time periods, or speakers.

```{webr}
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)
library(quanteda.textplots)

# Simulated broadsheet vs tabloid word distributions
set.seed(1)
broadsheet_words <- sample(
  c("parliament", "legislation", "scrutiny", "evidence", "policy",
    "minister", "committee", "report", "analysis", "government"),
  200, replace = TRUE
)
tabloid_words <- sample(
  c("star", "shock", "fury", "crisis", "drama",
    "blunder", "slammed", "row", "ban", "hit"),
  200, replace = TRUE
)

make_doc <- function(words, n = 200) {
  paste(sample(words, n, replace = TRUE), collapse = " ")
}

corp <- corpus(c(
  broadsheet = make_doc(broadsheet_words),
  tabloid    = make_doc(tabloid_words)
))

dfmat <- corp |>
  tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) |>
  dfm()

textstat_keyness(dfmat, target = "broadsheet") |>
  textplot_keyness()
```

## Collocations

Collocations reveal which words habitually co-occur — important for identifying fixed phrases like *fake news* or *climate crisis*.

```{webr}
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)

texts <- c(
  "fake news has become a widespread concern in media studies.",
  "climate crisis coverage dominated the news agenda last year.",
  "fake news allegations were repeated by the opposition party.",
  "the climate crisis requires urgent policy responses from governments.",
  "media studies scholars analysed fake news during the election."
)

toks <- corpus(texts) |>
  tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower()

textstat_collocations(toks, size = 2, min_count = 2)
```

## Temporal Trends

```{webr}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

# Simulated monthly article counts mentioning key terms
set.seed(7)
trend_data <- tibble(
  month = rep(seq(as.Date("2023-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-01"), by = "month"), 2),
  term  = rep(c("climate crisis", "fake news"), each = 24),
  n     = c(
    round(cumsum(rnorm(24, 2, 3)) + 30),
    round(abs(rnorm(24, 20, 5)))
  )
)

ggplot(trend_data, aes(month, n, colour = term)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %Y", date_breaks = "3 months") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Article count", colour = "Term",
       title = "Monthly term frequency, 2023–2024") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```
